sentences.text: We were talking about being back in Plaszow and watching your father going away.
And [SIGHS] of course, they had no work for us.
But they had to do something with us.
They put us in barracks.
And I never forget the middle of the night, I was covered with bed bugs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I am scared of any animal, any insect, or anything.
So I risked my life.
I took out the-- I went out from the barracks and I slept outside the barracks on the ground, because I couldn't take the bed bugs.
I was covered from top to bottom.
So they put us in barracks.
And of course, they didn't have work for us.
They didn't know what to do with us.
Who were you in the barracks with this time?
Were you with your mother and sister?
I was-- I was with my mother and my sister.

And I had a younger sister, which I had to protect her too, I mean cover up, because wherever we work you had to push out so-- we worked in a brush factory for a while, so you had to push out so many, so many brushes.
And she was a child, so she couldn't.
So I helped her with it.
It was constant fear.
You didn't know.
And in that brush factory where we were working, there was a special like cemetery.
They used to bring Jews from-- who were hiding on Aryan papers in Krakow, and they caught them.
So they used to bring them and the children, and they were killing them.
They had to undress, put down their clothes on the ground, and jump to that grave.
So we seen those actions almost every day, how they were killed because they were hiding, they ran away from camp, or the families gave them out, Polish families and so on.
So this we were faced with.
Then we had to carry stones, big stones, like in the Romans' times, big stones from one side of camp to the other side of camp.
For what reason?
Just we walk in that heat with those heavy stones lined up and bring from one side camp to another.
And then, many times we had to sit and break the stones with the hammers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And also, they were covering their crimes.
We had to dig out graves of the people who were shot in such a big graves or separate graves.
And we used to carry the bodies on a stack of boards, and then they burned them.
And I could never look at those bodies.
I used to-- once German hit me.
He saw I closed my eyes, because you had to look at it really.
So he hit me with a leather thing, a [INAUDIBLE], whatever they call it, you know, the whip, with a leather whip.
He hit me.
You open your eyes, look at it, because I used to go pick them out and close my eyes.
And-- and we carried them.
And that stink in camp, you can't imagine what the smell of burned body-- bodies gives.
It was just terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [INAUDIBLE] or whatever it was, unbelievable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was our work.
Dig out the graves, burn the bodies, carry the stones.
And like I said before, we were building a house, which we never finished, which they wanted to kill us.
This happened after we got back.
Until we left-- and of course, every day they lined you up, and they count you, and they killed you, and they shoot you.
And you tried to hide, cover up.
They shouldn't see that you're too young.
Always, this was in back of my mind with my sister.
And then at the end, we're hearing people going to Brunnlitz, to Schindler.
And my mother start running to the officials that we belong to go to Brunnlitz.
As far I remember, I thought we weren't on the list.
But I was wrong, because my mother said they took us off the list.
We are three people.
But she was running and begging, so they put us back on.
To whom, I don't know.
I cannot tell you.
So when they-- she came the last day, she says we're going to Brunnlitz.
I think three months.
No.
Plaszow, I didn't see Schindler at all.
Plaszow was a tremendous camp.
You didn't know if you were coming or going.
It was impossible.
We went, got back on the list.
So we went.
We were so thrilled that we're going to Brunnlitz.
And meanwhile, we wind up in Auschwitz.
300 women.
To Brunnlitz.
And I remember I used to put on 10 pairs-- not 10, 4 pairs of panties on me, 2 blouses, because we're going to Brunnlitz.
And meanwhile the train stopped--
Oh, you were packed like sardines, you know, in the cattle cars.
And a German was laughing, a soldier, I remember.
They stopped.
We went to urinate-- or already we were there.
I don't know.
So he was laughing, saw how many pair of panties I had on me.
Like to say, they're going to take it away.
They'll kill you anyway.
So we--
Train, and we wind up in Auschwitz.
No, I mean on the way, though, they would let you out to--
I think so.
Either when we came into Auschwitz or before, I don't remember.
Don't go by me, because I really don't remember too much.
And we got to Auschwitz.
Oh, this was a scene I don't think will ever leave my memory.
That's-- I felt like jumping on the electric wires to commit suicide.
First, it was I think a rainy night.
And we saw the smoke coming up from chimneys, from the ovens.
And the smell, of-- of course, too, of the bodies.
So people told us, they burning now 20,000 Hungarian troops.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what they told us.
And they took us to a big room [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the-- by the ovens.
And we had our luggage little bit with us, and we tried to sit on the luggage and to save.
So people pass by.
He says, what you're saving?
You're going to die.
You're-- you're going to be burned.
And we knew that we're going to the ovens.
We are being burnt.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And [SIGHS] then they told us to strip completely naked.
And they gave us haircuts.
I don't recall if they shaved our heads, or they got very short hair.
I don't recall at this.
And-- and they check us, and these are, you're going to the crematorium.
And they led us into one room.
And people start screaming, they smell gas.
We were sure we'll be cremated.
So all of a sudden, few drops of water came out from the-- from the faucets.
That scene what he made of Auschwitz, it's so true, if you look at the scene.
In the movie?
In the movie.
We went wild.
We went crazy.
We couldn't believe it that few drops of water came out, that we are safe, we aren't being burnt.
Yeah, with the whole group was together.
Oh yeah.
We knew we going to be killed.
And that's all.
No-- no two ways about it.
You were faced with the death every day, but here for sure.
And we saw bodies lying outside from crematoriums.
We saw all that.
And the people who worked there, they were-- they were like crazy.
And you cannot blame them.
They said, what you brought the luggage here?
You will be all cremated anyhow.
So they were hardened to everything by then?
Oh yeah.
Oh yeah.
You had to.
Yeah.
You had to.
And we came out.
No.
This was terrible.
All along I was in Auschwitz I wanted to commit suicide because was terrible there.
The hunger and everything, it was just terrible.
And the cold, and we had no clothes.
So when we came out from the showers, they threw to us clothes.
Oh yeah.
Yeah.
But then you were thinking you're going to suffer again.
And I always had a dream to have a whole loaf of bread in front of me.
I should be able to cut as much bread as I want that I could eat.
So hungry that you can't imagine.
But l looked good.
[LAUGHS] With all the hunger, I wasn't too skinny.
And they threw us clothes.
I got a French crinoline.
We looked like on a Purim.
And when we look at each other-- I think they'd shaved us, our hair.
I don't remember.
When we look at each other, we were hysterical how everybody look, how comical, how funny the clothes were they gave us.
And then, they put us in the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we didn't get one word of encouragement that we'll be alive, only temporary.
In those barracks, they have your toiletry, the bowl of soup what they gave you.
The bowl you used for your toilet and you used to eat your soup.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the hunger was unbelievable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was cold-- we had no clothes.
And every morning, they count us.
Always they were counting us.
Then I was pulled out from the line.
They called this appell where they're counting us.
A German came over and pulled me out, and pulled two or three people besides me.
And we were sure this is our-- I would look at my mother, and I said this is-- we-- they pulled me to go to the ovens.
Meanwhile, they took me to the hospital.
And because I didn't look too bad, like I told you, always that's my nature since the baby.
I've been always on the heavy side.
And took me to the hospital.
And the doctor came over, and he was really kind.
And he says, don't worry, I won't hurt you.
But we needs blood for the soldiers who are on the front.
They are dying.
And we need blood transfusions for them.
That time, Jewish blood was good for them-- wasn't anymore rassenschande.
Rassenschande, because they said we are a different race.
You cannot mix with Jews.
But that blood wasn't considered bad blood, because this was saving the wounded soldiers from the front.
They took from me a bottle of-- like a bottle of milk, so much blood.
And he says, you'll get extra portion of bread for it.
I got off that bed, but I was sure they're going to make experiments on me.
You didn't know what to expect, the terrible fear.
So I never got the bread, by the way.
They owe me.
And-- and the minute I got off that bed, I pass out, because they took so much blood out of me.
And right away I broke out.
My whole arms and whole thighs were covered with, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not blisters, what you call with the pus?
The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] boils.
My whole leg, thighs, and my arms were covered with-- with boils.
And boils, you know how this hurts, because they come with the pus and they draw.
Because they took out  all the blood out of me.
But, I survived.
No.
They didn't keep me in the hospital.
You couldn't complain that you're sick.
God forbid, if you were sick, they took you to the oven.
You had to be healthy.
But these were pains.
I even came to Brunnlitz I had them yet, crucial pains.
But I was lucky.
They brought me back to the barracks.
I don't know.
[SIGHING] I don't know what kept me going.
I really don't know, because you didn't think you're going to survive really.
You never thought that you'll be alive, that you're going to-- I was praying to die.
I will be honest with you.
How much suffering can a person take?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was terrible, the hunger, the cold, and everything else.
And the boils on top of it didn't help the situation.
Did you see Mengele at all?
Yeah.
When we had inspections, he was always there, no question about that.
He pull out people to the ovens.
If you had a pimple or you didn't look right to him, he dragged them out to the ovens.
And the last selection was when we were leaving Auschwitz.
So I always was with my mother and my sister.
We were staying always together.
Mengele pulled out my sister to go to the oven, because he found a pimple on her neck, or this, or maybe she was too young.
Was everyone naked during these inspections?
I-- no.
We were ready to got to the trains, so he pulled-- must have pulled her out because she-- she looked young.
She was young.
It depends.
It depends.
Sometimes in clothes, sometimes not.
So he pulled my sister out.
And my mother ran after her to go to the ovens.
Then from under the earth a German woman came and took them by the hand and pushed them to my side.
Either this was because they knew Schindler's Jews aren't allowed to be touched-- and I think that's why it happened.
The woman was standing by the selection and she pushed them back on the line to go on the train to Brunnlitz.
Yeah.
I still had the boils.
No.
We were dressed to go to the trains to leave for Brunnlitz.
This happened in the last minute.
We were all ready to go in my French crinoline.
[LAUGHS]
Three weeks we were.
I didn't see him.
But like it says, he took us out.
He bribed them with diamonds.
Otherwise, we would never leave Auschwitz.
300 women.
I don't know.
This I don't know.
I really don't know.
By train.
Cattle car too.
Oh, it was hell on Earth.
And we came to Brunnlitz at--
I don't remember.
I really don't.
We couldn't see nothing through the trains, because these were cattle cars.
When it opened when you got there.
Oh.
We got to Brunnlitz.
And I-- I think Schindler greeted us if I am not mistaken.
I am not sure.
I am not sure.
And we felt like we are back in heaven.
We-- there were Germans, of course, and German women who supposed to take care of us.
But we walked to that camp.
The room was warm where we were all living.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we felt relief.
That was one big room.
One big room.
For-- for the 300 women.
We slept one next to each other.
While I was there, I work in the-- what he built, the ammunition factory.
So I was working at the machine.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] makes too much noise?
Then-- then, at night, then I-- originally where he built this factory, this was a factory with wool.
So we had a lot of wool on the premises, wool yarns.
And a woman taught me how to knit.
And everybody came without clothes, so I used to knit.
For a man's sweater, I was getting a pound of bread.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And took me two nights to make a man's sweater.
I used to come at night.
I used-- I make a sweater for my mother, for my sister, and for people, which I used to get the extra bread, 'til I got this heavy infection in my eyes that's completely closed up.
I couldn't see.
I couldn't get up for work or anything.
So people went to Schindler and told him about me.
And he went out, bought medication in town.
And they brought it to me to save my eyesight.
Did you see him personally during that time?
I saw him, but I never talked to him.
I never got near him or anything like that.
No, I would be afraid to go over to-- whom am I to go thank him?
But the people who appealed in that or from the clinic, I don't recall how it happened.
And he saved my eyesight.
I used to see him coming to check-- I always work on the night shift.
Why?
Because here on the night shift, late at night-- in the evenings I used to knit, then I used to go on the night shift because I used to get extra plate of soup.
[SIGHS] They were-- you were hungry.
You were hungry, but maybe other people were-- were near Schindler, or they knew, whom you knew too, you know, people in kitchen.
But we were very helpless.
Otherwise, I wouldn't have knit those sweaters.
But you could live on the ration.
I mean you didn't die, but you could live on it.
Oh yeah.
He gained 700 places they gained in Plaszow.
When the men were sent away?
Were sent to-- to-- to Mauthausen.
This I remember, my mother-- he was shocked, Schindler, when he saw different men.
And he says, where are my men?
And my mother went over to Schindler to bring my father.
And he-- he had the list to bring the men back.
From Mauthausen.
But once he got there-- Mauthausen was the main concentration camp.
And they had little camps, so everyone was sent to a different camp.
Ebensee-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot the names.
They had quite a few-- Melk-- quite a few little camps.
So he couldn't find them anywhere, so he didn't bring anybody from Mauthausen, but he tried to bring it.
Of course.
It was a matter of life and death.
And Madritsch was a guy who was good too to Jewish people, German-- he put some people of his on the list also.
But it was a matter life and death.
And whatever people did, you cannot blame them.
To save your life, you'll do anything.
Mostly money played a big part, and whom you knew too, and money.
People had jewelry and so on.
No.
We had no contact.
No nothing.
We didn't know.
But we meet up with my father-- when the war ends, we're going to meet in Krakow.
That was our-- if the war ever ends, we meet in Krakow.
But we didn't know nothing.
People were saying, you know, all kinds of rumors.
People were saying, they are very close, they are very close.
That's what you heard.
The war will be over soon.
Yeah, '44.
The-- the war will end and everything.
And then, while I was in Brunnlitz, I know they said that they're going to kill us May 1st.
And we're digging our graves.
We suppose--
In Brunnlitz, yeah.
We supposed to be killed on May 1st.
That's what I remember.
On May 1st.
And I remember-- why do I remember, because I cried so that my birthday's May 1st, that on my 16th birthday, I'll be shot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Meanwhile, I was-- like I had said, I was working on the night shift.
And I saw Schindler coming.
We had a lagerfuhrer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a lagerfuhrer, a German.
He was a Gestapo.
So I saw Schindler walking with him, and he was drunk.
He couldn't walk straight.
And next day that lagerfuhrer disappeared.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how he got rid of him.
That's the way I remember.
What do you think happened?
Either the partisan-- because people were in touch with the underground, Czechoslovakian underground, either they got rid of him.
I think he got rid of him.
How and what, I--
Oh yeah.
He got him drunk and he walked him through that place where we work.
And he disappeared and we were saved.
Yeah, purposely did.
This he did.
How and what, I heard rumors, but they were rumors.
Somebody said he put him on a carriage and the partisans got rid of him.
Other people said that gave him a strong letter, he has to go to Berlin.
I don't know exactly.
But he disappeared, and we'd been saved.
And I saw him.
He was so-- he couldn't walk straight.
And when the war ended, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't know the war ended even.
Through the gates from the camp, I saw Germans running away with white flags.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We knew we going to be free.
Schindler disappeared.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were left like alone without any power, without anybody.
And I think I would say two days after the war ended, a Russian soldier came in on a horse [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and told us, you are liberated.
Oh.
We kissed him.
[LAUGHS] We kissed him that you can't imagine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they were such animals, the Russians, when they walked in, so we didn't know which we were better off.
